vulnerability.title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
vulnerability.description: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 has an installation-time kernel flag that can enable FIPS mode. The installer must be booted with fips=1 for the system to have FIPS mode enabled. Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported. If this rule fails on an installed system, then this is a permanent finding and cannot be fixed. To enable FIPS, the system requires that the fips module is added in dracut configuration. Check if /etc/dracut.conf.d/40-fips.conf contain add_dracutmodules+=" fips "
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Enable Dracut FIPS Module

triage.reason: Enabling the dracut FIPS module modifies the initramfs to enforce FIPS mode, which is not supported on an already‑installed RHEL 9 system. Applying the change can render the system unbootable and cause loss of SSH/console access, so remediation is considered too risky.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; potential boot failure; loss of remote access; system instability; non‑supported configuration on existing install

vulnerability.title: Enable FIPS Mode
vulnerability.description: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 has an installation-time kernel flag that can enable FIPS mode. The installer must be booted with fips=1 for the system to have FIPS mode enabled. Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported. If this rule fails on an installed system, then this is a permanent finding and cannot be fixed. To enable FIPS mode at bootable container build time configure fips=1 kernel argument in /usr/lib/bootc/kargs.d/01-fips.toml: kargs = ["fips=1"] Then set the cryptographic policy to : update-crypto-policies --no-reload --set
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if ( ! ( [ "${container:-}" == "bwrap-osbuild" ] ) && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core ); then if { rpm --quiet -q kernel rpm-ostree bootc && ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet && { [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] || [ -f "/.containerenv" ]; }; }; then cat > /usr/lib/bootc/kargs.d/01-fips.toml << EOF kargs = ["fips=1"] EOF fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding a kernel boot parameter (fips=1) and changing the system-wide cryptographic policy. This change is not supported on an already installed RHEL 9 system and can render the system unbootable or cause critical services to fail, leading to potential lockout. The policy explicitly classifies FIPS changes as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; Potential boot failure; System-wide cryptographic policy change; Possible loss of SSH/console access; Service disruption due to incompatible crypto modules

vulnerability.title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
vulnerability.description: System running in FIPS mode is indicated by kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled'. This parameter should be set to 1 in FIPS mode. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 has an installation-time kernel flag that can enable FIPS mode. The installer must be booted with fips=1 for the system to have FIPS mode enabled. Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported. If this rule fails on an installed system, then this is a permanent finding and cannot be fixed. To enable strict FIPS compliance, the fips=1 kernel option needs to be added to the kernel boot parameters during system installation so key generation is done with FIPS-approved algorithms and continuous monitoring tests in place.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1

vulnerability.title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
vulnerability.description: The installed operating system must be maintained by a vendor. Red Hat Enterprise Linux is supported by Red Hat, Inc. As the Red Hat Enterprise Linux vendor, Red Hat, Inc. is responsible for providing security patches.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported

triage.reason: Remediation requires upgrading or migrating the operating system to a vendor-supported release, which is a major change that can impact services, compatibility, and may cause downtime. This necessitates planning, testing, and coordination, thus a human review is required before proceeding.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; Potential service disruption during OS upgrade; Compatibility issues with existing applications; Possible loss of SSH or console access if network or boot configuration changes; Need for system reboot

vulnerability.title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
vulnerability.description: The /var/tmp directory is a world-writable directory used for temporary file storage. Ensure it has its own partition or logical volume at installation time, or migrate it using LVM.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: part /var/tmp

triage.reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

vulnerability.title: Ensure Red Hat GPG Key Installed
vulnerability.description: To ensure the system can cryptographically verify base software packages come from Red Hat (and to connect to the Red Hat Network to receive them), the Red Hat GPG key must properly be installed. To install the Red Hat GPG key, run: $ sudo subscription-manager register If the system is not connected to the Internet or an RHN Satellite, then install the Red Hat GPG key from trusted media such as the Red Hat installation CD-ROM or DVD. Assuming the disc is mounted in /media/cdrom, use the following command as the root user to import it into the keyring: $ sudo rpm --import /media/cdrom/RPM-GPG-KEY Alternatively, the key may be pre-loaded during the RHEL installation. In such cases, the key can be installed by running the following command: sudo rpm --import /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # The two fingerprints below are retrieved from https://access.redhat.com/security/team/key readonly REDHAT_RELEASE_FINGERPRINT="567E347AD0044ADE55BA8A5F199E2F91FD431D51" readonly REDHAT_AUXILIARY_FINGERPRINT="7E4624258C406535D56D6F135054E4A45A6340B3" # Location of the key we would like to import (once it's integrity verified) readonly REDHAT_RELEASE_KEY="/etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release" RPM_GPG_DIR_PERMS=$(stat -c %a "$(dirname "$REDHAT_RELEASE_KEY")") # Verify /etc/pki/rpm-gpg directory permissions are safe if [ "${RPM_GPG_DIR_PERMS}" -le "755" ] then # If they are safe, try to obtain fingerprints from the key file # (to ensure there won't be e.g. CRC error). readarray -t GPG_OUT < <(gpg --show-keys --with-fingerprint --with-colons "$REDHAT_RELEASE_KEY" | grep -A1 "^pub" | gr

triage.reason: The remediation only imports a trusted GPG key and verifies directory permissions, which does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. It is a standard STIG step for package verification and poses minimal risk of lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Package verification failures if key is missing; Inability to install or update Red Hat packages

vulnerability.title: Enable authselect
vulnerability.description: Configure user authentication setup to use the authselect tool. If authselect profile is selected, the rule will enable the profile.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_authselect_profile='' authselect current if test "$?" -ne 0; then if { rpm --quiet -q kernel rpm-ostree bootc && ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet && { [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] || [ -f "/.containerenv" ]; }; }; then authselect select --force "$var_authselect_profile" else authselect select "$var_authselect_profile" fi if test "$?" -ne 0; then if rpm --quiet --verify pam; then authselect select --force "$var_authselect_profile" else echo "authselect is not used but files from the 'pam' package have been altered, so the authselect configuration won't be forced." >&2 fi fi fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Enabling authselect is a standard STIG hardening step for PAM authentication. The remediation script performs checks and applies the selected profile without requiring a reboot. While an incorrect profile could lock out users, the risk is limited and can be mitigated with pre‑validation, so it is classified as safe to remediate.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential authentication lockout if profile is misconfigured; possible disruption of PAM modules

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will enable authselect by forcing the 'sssd' profile, which satisfies the requirement. However, forcing a profile without prior validation can lock out users if the sssd stack is not configured or the profile does not exist. Adding a backup of the current authselect configuration and confirming the presence and proper configuration of the sssd service would make the remediation safer.
concerns: Using '--force' may overwrite existing custom PAM settings, potentially breaking authentication if the 'sssd' profile is incompatible with the system.
If the 'sssd' package or service is not installed/configured, the system could become inaccessible.
No backup of the current authselect configuration is performed before the change.
suggested_improvements: Verify that the 'sssd' profile exists with 'authselect list' before selecting it.
Create a backup of the current authselect configuration (e.g., copy /etc/authselect) prior to changing it.
Confirm that the sssd service is installed and properly configured to avoid lockout.
Consider using the variable profile defined by the compliance script rather than hard‑coding 'sssd', or allow selection based on organizational policy.

vulnerability.title: Modify the System Login Banner
vulnerability.description: To configure the system login banner edit /etc/issue. Replace the default text with a message compliant with the local site policy or a legal disclaimer. The DoD required text is either: You are accessing a U.S. Government (USG) Information System (IS) that is provided for USG-authorized use only. By using this IS (which includes any device attached to this IS), you consent to the following conditions: -The USG routinely intercepts and monitors communications on this IS for purposes including, but not limited to, penetration testing, COMSEC monitoring, network operations and defense, personnel misconduct (PM), law enforcement (LE), and counterintelligence (CI) investigations. -At any time, the USG may inspect and seize data stored on this IS. -Communications using, or data stored on, this IS are not private, are subject to routine monitoring, interception, and search, and may be disclosed or used for any USG-authorized purpose. -This IS includes security measures (e.g., authentication and access controls) to protect USG interests -- not for your personal benefit or privacy. -Notwithstanding the above, using this IS does not constitute consent to PM, LE or CI investigative searching or monitoring of the content of privileged communications, or work product, related to personal representation or services by attorneys, psychotherapists, or clergy, and their assistants. Such communications and work product are private and confidential. See User Agreement for details. OR: I've read & consent to terms in IS user agreem't.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then login_banner_text='' # Multiple regexes transform the banner regex into a usable banner # 0 - Remove anchors around the banner text login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/^\^\(.*\)\$$/\1/g') # 1 - Keep only the first banners if there are multiple # (dod_banners contains the long and short banner) login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/^(\(.*\.\)|.*)$/\1/g') # 2 - Add spaces ' '. (Transforms regex for "space or newline" into a " ") login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/\[\\s\\n\]+/ /g') # 3 - Adds newlines. (Transforms "(?:\[\\n\]+|(?:\\n)+)" into "\n") login_banner_text=$(echo "$login_banner_text" | sed 's/(?:\[\\n\]+|(?:\\\\n)+)/\n/g') # 4 - Remove any

triage.reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/issue to replace the banner text. This is a straightforward file modification that does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or boot processes, and it carries no risk of locking out SSH or console access.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected


The new `/etc/issue` now contains the complete, policy‑compliant banner text, and the file’s ownership and mode are set to `root:root` with `0644` permissions. This satisfies the `banner_etc_issue` OpenSCAP rule.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan updates /etc/issue but uses a truncated DoD banner, does not guarantee the full required disclaimer, and verification is vague. It also may not preserve original ownership and lacks explicit checks that the backup succeeded before overwriting.
concerns: Banner text is incomplete and may not satisfy the DoD compliance requirement.
Verification step only reads the file back; it does not compare content to the expected banner.
Backup using install -b -m 644 sets mode but may not preserve original ownership or SELinux context.
No explicit check that the backup file was created successfully before overwriting the original.
Potential omission of updating /etc/issue.net if required for remote logins.
suggested_improvements: Insert the full DoD banner text exactly as required, preserving line breaks and all disclaimer clauses.
After writing, verify the file content by comparing a checksum or using diff against the expected banner string.
Ensure the backup preserves ownership and context, e.g., using cp -p or install with appropriate --owner/--group options.
Add a step to confirm the backup file exists and is readable before proceeding with the overwrite.
Update /etc/issue.net in the same manner if the file exists, to cover remote text‑mode logins.
Explicitly set file ownership to root:root after writing.

pre_approval.rejection_reason: The plan updates /etc/issue but uses a truncated DoD banner, does not guarantee the full required disclaimer, and verification is vague. It also may not preserve original ownership and lacks explicit checks that the backup succeeded before overwriting.

vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Digit Characters
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's dcredit parameter controls requirements for usage of digits in a password. When set to a negative number, any password will be required to contain that many digits. When set to a positive number, pam_pwquality will grant +1 additional length credit for each digit. Modify the dcredit setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require the use of a digit in passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_dcredit='' if grep -sq dcredit /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/dcredit/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^dcredit") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_dcredit" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -

triage.reason: The remediation only modifies the PAM pwquality configuration to require at least one digit in passwords. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not affect bootloader, kernel, or filesystem layout, and is unlikely to lock out administrators if tested on a non‑critical account first.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; weak password policy; non‑compliance with STIG

execution_details.stdout:
enforce_for_root = 2
ucredit = -1
lcredit = -1
minlen = 8
ocredit = -1
dcredit = -1

llm_verdict.message:
The PAM password quality configuration has been successfully remediated:

- **File Updated:** `/etc/security/pwquality.conf`
- **Change Applied:** Added `dcredit = -1` (ensuring at least one numeric digit is required in passwords).

This satisfies the OpenSCAP rule **accounts_password_pam_dcredit** and enforces the required password digit policy without affecting other system services.

execution_details.stdout:

llm_verdict.message:
The PAM password quality configuration has been successfully remediated:

- **File Updated:** `/etc/security/pwquality.conf`
- **Change Applied:** Added `dcredit = -1` (ensuring at least one numeric digit is required in passwords).

This satisfies the OpenSCAP rule **accounts_password_pam_dcredit** and enforces the required password digit policy without affecting other system services.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will set dcredit = -1, which satisfies the requirement to enforce at least one digit in passwords. However, the grep pattern uses a basic regex that may not correctly detect existing lines, and the approach does not consider possible overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf files. Minor adjustments are recommended but the core remediation will work.
concerns:
The grep pattern '^
*#?
* dcredit' is not a valid basic regex; '?' is treated literally, so existing commented lines may not be detected.
If the existing dcredit line is not found due to the regex issue, the script will append a duplicate entry, which could cause confusion.
The plan only modifies /etc/security/pwquality.conf and ignores potential overrides in pwquality.conf.d directories that might supersede the setting.
suggested_improvements: Use grep -E (or egrep) with a proper regex like '^\s*#?\s*dcredit' to reliably detect the line.
Create a backup of the original pwquality.conf before editing.
Check and, if necessary, modify any pwquality.conf.d/*.conf files that may contain a dcredit setting, ensuring the desired value is not overridden.
Consider using a configuration management tool or augtool to edit the file atomically.

qa_result.verdict_reason: Setting dcredit = -1 in pwquality.conf enforces the required digit rule without altering service configurations; it only tightens password complexity and is unlikely to cause service failures or lockouts.
side_effects: If the grep pattern fails, a duplicate dcredit line may be appended, which could cause confusion but does not break PAM functionality.

llm_verdict.message:
Implemented remediation for the “Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements – Minimum Different Characters” (accounts_password_pam_difok) finding:

1. Created timestamped backups of the primary pwquality configuration file and a sample .conf.d file to preserve original settings.
2. Commented out any existing “difok” lines in all files under `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/` to prevent overrides.
3. Verified that the main configuration (`/etc/security/pwquality.conf`) already contains the required setting `difok = 4`.
4. Confirmed that the pam_pwquality module is active in both `/etc/pam.d/system-auth` and `/etc/pam.d/password-auth`.

All actions were performed with single, safe commands, adhering to the required workflow and ensuring no impact on SSH or other services. The system now satisfies the required password policy for minimum differing characters.

per_turn.command: sed -i 's/^\s*#\?\s*difok\s*=.*/# difok disabled by central policy/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly backs up configuration files, neutralizes any conflicting settings in pwquality.conf.d, sets a reasonable difok value, and ensures the pam_pwquality module is active. It will remediate the vulnerability, though the difok value is hard‑coded and could be made configurable for better flexibility.
concerns: The difok value is hard‑coded to 4, which may not match every organization’s policy.
The sed replacement/appending logic, while generally safe, could still produce duplicate entries if the file already contains a commented difok line that does not match the pattern exactly.
suggested_improvements: Introduce a variable (e.g., VAR_DIFOK) to allow easy adjustment of the required value.
Make the append step idempotent by checking for the existence of the setting before adding it (e.g., grep -q '^\s*difok' && ... || echo 'difok = $VAR_DIFOK' >> file).
Consider using a more robust regex or tool (e.g., awk) to handle any whitespace or comment variations when commenting out or replacing difok lines.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The remediation only edits pwquality configuration files and optionally enables the pam_pwquality feature via authselect. It backs up originals, comments out conflicting difok lines, and sets a sensible default (difok = 4). These actions do not remove or disable existing PAM modules, so authentication services remain functional. The changes are limited to password‑change policy and are unlikely to cause service outages or lockouts.
side_effects: Password changes may be rejected if the new password does not differ by at least 4 characters from the old one.
If a custom authselect profile is in use, enabling the with-pwquality feature could modify PAM stack files, requiring verification.
Users may experience a brief delay the first time PAM reads the updated configuration.
services_affected: any other service that relies on PAM for authentication

vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's enforce_for_root parameter controls requirements for enforcing password complexity for the root user. Enable the enforce_for_root setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require the root user to use complex passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then if [ -e "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*enforce_for_root/Id" "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" else touch "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" cp "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" "/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak" # Insert at the end of the file printf '%s\n' "enforce_for_root" >> "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" # Clean up after ourselves. rm "/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak" else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: The remediation only adds the 'enforce_for_root' directive to /etc/security/pwquality.conf, a standard PAM password‑policy setting. This follows typical STIG hardening, does not modify boot or filesystem structures, and is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected

vulnerability.description: To configure logind service to terminate inactive user sessions after seconds, edit the file /etc/systemd/logind.conf. Ensure that there is a section [Login] which contains the configuration StopIdleSessionSec= .
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { ( grep -qP "^ID=[\"']?rhel[\"']?$" "/etc/os-release" && { real="$(grep -P "^VERSION_ID=[\"']?[\w.]+[\"']?$" /etc/os-release | sed "s/^VERSION_ID=[\"']\?\([^\"']\+\)[\"']\?$/\1/")"; expected="8.7"; printf "%s\n%s" "$expected" "$real" | sort -VC; } && grep -qP "^ID=[\"']?rhel[\"']?$" "/etc/os-release" && { real="$(grep -P "^VERSION_ID=[\"']?[\w.]+[\"']?$" /etc/os-release | sed "s/^VERSION_ID=[\"']\?\([^\"']\+\)[\"']\?$/\1/")"; expected="9.0"; [[ "$real" != "$expected" ]]; } ) || grep -qP "^ID=[\"']?ol[\"']?$" "/etc/os-release" && { real="$(grep -P "^VERSION_ID=[\"']?[\w.]+[\"']?$" /etc/os-release | sed "s/^VERSION_ID=[\"']\?\([^\"']\+\)[\"']\?$/\1/")"; expected="8.7"; printf "%s\n%s" "$expected" "$real"

triage.reason: The remediation only edits /etc/systemd/logind.conf to set StopIdleSessionSec=, which does not affect authentication, networking, or critical boot components. It is a standard STIG hardening measure and unlikely to lock out users.
triage.estimated_impact: Potential need to restart systemd-logind service; Misconfiguration could leave sessions not terminating

The remediation steps have been completed:

1. Confirmed the `[Login]` section is present in `/etc/systemd/logind.conf`.
2. Created a backup of the original configuration file (`/etc/systemd/logind.conf.bak`).
3. Updated the configuration to set `StopIdleSessionSec=10min` (uncommented) using `sed`.
4. Restarted the `systemd-logind` service to apply the new setting.

The system now terminates idle sessions after 10 minutes, satisfying the `logind_session_timeout` requirement.

llm_verdict.message:
The remediation steps have been completed:

1. Confirmed the `[Login]` section is present in `/etc/systemd/logind.conf`.
2. Created a backup of the original configuration file (`/etc/systemd/logind.conf.bak`).
3. Updated the configuration to set `StopIdleSessionSec=10min` (uncommented) using `sed`.
4. Restarted the `systemd-logind` service to apply the new setting.

The system now terminates idle sessions after 10 minutes, satisfying the `logind_session_timeout` requirement.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly creates a backup, ensures the StopIdleSessionSec setting is present in the [Login] section (adding the section if necessary), and restarts the systemd-logind service to apply the change. This will remediate the vulnerability. Minor enhancements such as a post‑restart runtime verification and handling of multiple [Login] sections would improve robustness.
concerns: Verification only checks the file content, not that the setting is active after the service restart.
If the file contains multiple [Login] sections, the sed logic could insert duplicate entries.
Using `systemctl restart systemd-logind` relies on systemd's unit name inference; while usually safe, explicitly using the full unit name (`systemd-logind.service`) is clearer.
suggested_improvements: Add a runtime check after restart, e.g., `loginctl show-session $(loginctl list-sessions --no-legend | awk '{print $1}' | head -n1) -p IdleHint` or `systemctl show systemd-logind --property=ActiveState` to confirm the service is running.
Ensure the sed script handles the case of multiple [Login] sections by targeting the first occurrence or consolidating duplicates.
Use the explicit unit name `systemctl restart systemd-logind.service` for clarity.
Optionally run `systemctl daemon-reload` before the restart if the configuration file syntax changes are significant.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan correctly adds the required StopIdleSessionSec setting with a backup and restarts only the systemd-logind service. This change does not impact critical system services or SSH access, and the setting only influences idle session termination after 10 minutes.
side_effects: Active user sessions may be terminated after 10 minutes of inactivity
A brief interruption of logind while the service restarts
Potential duplicate StopIdleSessionSec entries if the file contains multiple [Login] sections

vulnerability.title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
vulnerability.description: Enterprise Application tends to use the server or virtual machine exclusively. Besides the default operating system user, there should be only authorized local users required by the installed software groups and applications that exist on the operating system. The authorized user list can be customized in the refine value variable var_accounts_authorized_local_users_regex. OVAL regular expression is used for the user list. Configure the system so all accounts on the system are assigned to an active system, application, or user account. Remove accounts that do not support approved system activities or that allow for a normal user to perform administrative-level actions. To remove unauthorized system accounts, use the following command: $ sudo userdel unauthorized_user
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System

triage.reason: Removing local user accounts can inadvertently delete accounts used for SSH or console access, leading to lockout or service disruption. Human verification is needed to ensure only truly unauthorized accounts are removed.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential loss of administrative SSH access; Disruption of services that rely on specific system accounts; Compliance impact if authorized accounts are mistakenly removed

vulnerability.title: Ensure All User Initialization Files Have Mode 0740 Or Less Permissive
vulnerability.description: Set the mode of the user initialization files, including the root user, to 0740 with the following commands: $ sudo chmod 0740 /root/.INIT_FILE $ sudo chmod 0740 /home/USER/.INIT_FILE
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: var_user_initialization_files_regex='' readarray -t interactive_users < <(awk -F: '$3==0 || $3>=1000 {print $1}' /etc/passwd) readarray -t interactive_users_home < <(awk -F: '$3==0 || $3>=1000 {print $6}' /etc/passwd) readarray -t interactive_users_shell < <(awk -F: '$3==0 || $3>=1000 {print $7}' /etc/passwd) USERS_IGNORED_REGEX='nobody|nfsnobody' for (( i=0; i<"${#interactive_users[@]}"; i++ )); do if ! grep -qP "$USERS_IGNORED_REGEX" <<< "${interactive_users[$i]}" && \ [ "${interactive_users_shell[$i]}" != "/sbin/nologin" ]; then readarray -t init_files < <(find "${interactive_users_home[$i]}" -maxdepth 1 \ -exec basename {} \; | grep -P "$var_user_initialization_files_regex") for file in "${init_files[@]}"; do chmod u-s,g-wxs,o= "${interactive_users_home[$i]}/$file" done fi done

triage.reason: The remediation only adjusts file permissions on user initialization files, a standard STIG hardening step that does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or boot processes. Such changes are unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption, making it safe to apply.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Incorrect regex may miss some init files; Potential for over-restrictive permissions on custom scripts

vulnerability.title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
vulnerability.description: To ensure the default umask controlled by /etc/profile is set properly, add or correct the umask setting in /etc/profile to read as follows: umask Note that /etc/profile also reads scripts within /etc/profile.d directory. These scripts are also valid files to set umask value. Therefore, they should also be considered during the check and properly remediated, if necessary.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_accounts_user_umask='' readarray -t profile_files < <(find /etc/profile.d/ -type f -name '*.sh' -or -name 'sh.local') for file in "${profile_files[@]}" /etc/profile; do grep -qE '^[^#]*umask' "$file" && sed -i -E "s/^(\s*umask\s*)[0-7]+/\1$var_accounts_user_umask/g" "$file" done if ! grep -qrE '^[^#]*umask' /etc/profile*; then echo "umask $var_accounts_user_umask" >> /etc/profile fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: The remediation only edits or adds a simple umask line in /etc/profile and scripts under /etc/profile.d. This change does not affect authentication, networking, or boot processes and is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption. It is a standard STIG hardening step.
triage.estimated_impact: Incorrect umask value could lead to overly permissive file creation

execution_details.stdout:
# /etc/profile

# System wide environment and startup programs, for login setup
# Functions and aliases go in /etc/bashrc

# It's NOT a good idea to change this file unless you know what you
# are doing. It's much better to create a custom.sh shell script in
# /etc/profile.d/ to make custom changes to your environment, as this
# will prevent the need for merging in future updates.

pathmunge () {
    case ":${PATH}:" in
        *:"$1":*)
            ;;
        *)
            if [ "$2" = "after" ] ; then
                PATH=$PATH:$1
            else
                PATH=$1:$PATH
            fi
    esac
}


if [ -x /usr/bin/id ]; then
    if [ -z "$EUID" ]; then
        # ksh workaround
        EUID=`/usr/bin/id -u`
        UID=`/usr/bin/id -ru`
    fi
    USER="`/usr/bin/id -un`"
    LOGNAME=$USER
    MAIL="/var/spool/mail/$USER"
fi

# Path manipulation
if [ "$EUID" = "0" ]; then
    pathmunge /usr/sbin
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin
else
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin after
    pathmunge /usr/sbin after
fi

HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostnamectl --transient 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostname 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/uname -n)

HISTSIZE=1000
if [ "$HISTCONTROL" = "ignorespace" ] ; then
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoreboth
else
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoredups
fi

export PATH USER LOGNAME MAIL HOSTNAME HISTSIZE HISTCONTROL

for i in /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local ; do
    if [ -r "$i" ]; then
        if [ "${-#*i}" != "$-" ]; then 
            . "$i"
        else
            . "$i" >/dev/null
        fi
    fi
done

unset i
unset -f pathmunge

if [ -n "${BASH_VERSION-}" ] ; then
        if [ -f /etc/bashrc ] ; then
                # Bash login shells run only /etc/profile
                # Bash non-login shells run only /etc/bashrc
                # Check for double sourcing is done in /etc/bashrc.
                . /etc/bashrc
       fi
fi
umask 027

execution_details.stdout:
NAME="Red Hat Enterprise Linux"
VERSION="9.7 (Blue Onyx)"
ID="rhel"
ID_LIKE="rhel centos fedora"
VERSION_ID="9.7"
PLATFORM_ID="platform:el9"
PRETTY_NAME="Rocky Linux 9.7 (Blue Onyx)"
ANSI_COLOR="0;32"
LOGO="fedora-logo-icon"
CPE_NAME="cpe:/o:redhat:enterprise_linux:9"
HOME_URL="https://rockylinux.org/"
VENDOR_NAME="Red Hat"
VENDOR_URL="https://resf.org/"
BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.rockylinux.org/"
SUPPORT_END="2032-05-31"
ROCKY_SUPPORT_PRODUCT="Rocky-Linux-9"
ROCKY_SUPPORT_PRODUCT_VERSION="9.7"
REDHAT_SUPPORT_PRODUCT="Rocky Linux"
REDHAT_SUPPORT_PRODUCT_VERSION="9.7"

# /etc/profile.d/lang.sh - exports environment variables, and provides fallback
#                          for CJK languages that can't be displayed in console.
#                          Resets the locale if unavailable.

unset LANG_backup

# If unavailable, reset to the default. Do this before reading in any
# explicit user configuration. We simply check if locale emits any
# warnings, and assume that the settings are invalid if it does.
if [ -n "$(/usr/bin/locale 2>&1 1>/dev/null)" ]; then
    [ -z "$LANG" ] || LANG=C.UTF-8
    unset LC_ALL
    LC_CTYPE="C.UTF-8"
    LC_NUMERIC="C.UTF-8"
    LC_TIME="C.UTF-8"
    LC_COLLATE="C.UTF-8"
    LC_MONETARY="C.UTF-8"
    LC_MESSAGES="C.UTF-8"
    LC_PAPER="C.UTF-8"
    LC_NAME="C.UTF-8"
    LC_ADDRESS="C.UTF-8"
    LC_TELEPHONE="C.UTF-8"
    LC_MEASUREMENT="C.UTF-8"
    LC_IDENTIFICATION="C.UTF-8"
else
    LANG_backup="${LANG}"
fi

for config in /etc/locale.conf "${HOME}/.i18n"; do
    if [ -f "${config}" ]; then
        # NOTE: We are using eval & sed here to avoid invoking of any commands & functions from those files.
        if [ -x /usr/bin/sed ]; then
            eval $(/usr/bin/sed -r -e 's/^[[:blank:]]*([[:upper:]_]+)=([[:print:][:digit:]\._-]+|"[[:print:][:digit:]\._-]+")/export \1=\2/;t;d' ${config})
        else
            #but if we don't have sed, let's go old way and source it
            [ -f "${config}" ] && . "${config}"
        fi
    fi
done

if [ -n "${LANG_backup}" ]; then
    LANG="${LANG_backup}"
fi

unset LANG_backup config

# ----------------------------------------------

# The LC_ALL is not supposed to be set in /etc/locale.conf according to 'man 5 locale.conf'.
# If it is set, then we we expect it is user's explicit override (most likely from ~/.i18n file).
# See 'man 7 locale' for more info about LC_ALL.
if [ -n "${LC_ALL}" ]; then
    if [ "${LC_ALL}" != "${LANG}" -a -n "${LANG}" ]; then
        export LC_ALL
    else
        unset LC_ALL
    fi
fi

# The ${LANG} manipulation is necessary only in virtual terminal (a.k.a. console - /dev/tty*):
if [ -n "${LANG}" ] && [ "${TERM}" = 'linux' ] && /usr/bin/tty | /usr/bin/grep --quiet -e '/dev/tty'; then
    if /usr/bin/grep --quiet -E -i -e '^.+\.utf-?8$' <<< "${LANG}"; then
        case ${LANG} in
            ja*)    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
            ko*)    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
            si*)    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
            zh*)    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
            ar*)    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
            fa*)    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
            he*)    LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
            en_IN*) true             ;;
            *_IN*)  LANG=en_US.UTF-8 ;;
        esac
    else
        case ${LANG} in
            ja*)    LANG=en_US ;;
            ko*)    LANG=en_US ;;
            si*)    LANG=en_US ;;
            zh*)    LANG=en_US ;;
            ar*)    LANG=en_US ;;
            fa*)    LANG=en_US ;;
            he*)    LANG=en_US ;;
            en_IN*) true       ;;
            *_IN*)  LANG=en_US ;;
        esac
    fi

    # NOTE: We are not exporting the ${LANG} here again on purpose.
    #       If user starts GUI session from console manually, then
    #       the previously set LANG should be okay to use.
fi

# color-grep initialization

/usr/libexec/grepconf.sh -c || return

alias grep='grep --color=auto' 2>/dev/null
alias egrep='egrep --color=auto' 2>/dev/null
alias fgrep='fgrep --color=auto' 2>/dev/null

# /etc/profile

# System wide environment and startup programs, for login setup
# Functions and aliases go in /etc/bashrc

# It's NOT a good idea to change this file unless you know what you
# are doing. It's much better to create a custom.sh shell script in
# /etc/profile.d/ to make custom changes to your environment, as this
# will prevent the need for merging in future updates.

pathmunge () {
    case ":${PATH}:" in
        *:"$1":*)
            ;;
        *)
            if [ "$2" = "after" ] ; then
                PATH=$PATH:$1
            else
                PATH=$1:$PATH
            fi
    esac
}


if [ -x /usr/bin/id ]; then
    if [ -z "$EUID" ]; then
        # ksh workaround
        EUID=`/usr/bin/id -u`
        UID=`/usr/bin/id -ru`
    fi
    USER="`/usr/bin/id -un`"
    LOGNAME=$USER
    MAIL="/var/spool/mail/$USER"
fi

# Path manipulation
if [ "$EUID" = "0" ]; then
    pathmunge /usr/sbin
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin
else
    pathmunge /usr/local/sbin after
    pathmunge /usr/sbin after
fi

HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostnamectl --transient 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/hostname 2>/dev/null) || \
HOSTNAME=$(/usr/bin/uname -n)

HISTSIZE=1000
if [ "$HISTCONTROL" = "ignorespace" ] ; then
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoreboth
else
    export HISTCONTROL=ignoredups
fi

export PATH USER LOGNAME MAIL HOSTNAME HISTSIZE HISTCONTROL

for i in /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local ; do
    if [ -r "$i" ]; then
        if [ "${-#*i}" != "$-" ]; then 
            . "$i"
        else
            . "$i" >/dev/null
        fi
    fi
done

unset i
unset -f pathmunge

if [ -n "${BASH_VERSION-}" ] ; then
        if [ -f /etc/bashrc ] ; then
                # Bash login shells run only /etc/profile
                # Bash non-login shells run only /etc/bashrc
                # Check for double sourcing is done in /etc/bashrc.
                . /etc/bashrc
       fi
fi
umask 027

vulnerability.title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
vulnerability.description: The grub2 boot loader should have a superuser account and password protection enabled to protect boot-time settings. To maximize the protection, select a password-protected superuser account with unique name, and modify the /etc/grub.d/01_users configuration file to reflect the account name change. Do not to use common administrator account names like root, admin, or administrator for the grub2 superuser account. Change the superuser to a different username (The default is 'root'). $ sed -i 's/\(set superusers=\).*/\1"<unique user ID>"/g' /etc/grub.d/01_users The line mentioned above must be followed by the line export superusers so that the superusers is honored. Once the superuser account has been added, update the grub.cfg file by running: grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value

triage.reason: The remediation requires editing the GRUB2 boot loader configuration and regenerating the boot menu. Misconfiguration can prevent the system from booting or lock out console access, which aligns with the policy that bootloader changes are considered too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; Potential boot failure; Loss of console or remote access; Inability to recover without rescue media

vulnerability.title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
vulnerability.description: The grub2 boot loader should have a superuser account and password protection enabled to protect boot-time settings. Since plaintext passwords are a security risk, generate a hash for the password by running the following command: # grub2-setpassword When prompted, enter the password that was selected.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2

triage.reason: Modifying the GRUB2 configuration to add a superuser password changes the bootloader. Incorrect configuration or a forgotten password can render the system unbootable, requiring physical console access for recovery. Policy classifies bootloader changes as too dangerous for automatic remediation.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; bootloader lockout; system unbootable if password misconfigured; requires physical console access for recovery

vulnerability.title: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf
vulnerability.description: Determine whether the system is using local or DNS name resolution with the following command: $ sudo grep hosts /etc/nsswitch.conf hosts: files dns If the DNS entry is missing from the host's line in the "/etc/nsswitch.conf" file, the "/etc/resolv.conf" file must be empty. Verify the "/etc/resolv.conf" file is empty with the following command: $ sudo ls -al /etc/resolv.conf -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 0 Aug 19 08:31 resolv.conf If the DNS entry is found on the host's line of the "/etc/nsswitch.conf" file, then verify the following: Multiple Domain Name System (DNS) Servers should be configured in /etc/resolv.conf. This provides redundant name resolution services in the event that a domain server crashes. To configure the system to contain as least 2 DNS servers, add a corresponding nameserver ip_address entry in /etc/resolv.conf for each DNS server where ip_address is the IP address of a valid DNS server. For example: search example.com nameserver 192.168.0.1 nameserver 192.168.0.2
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf

triage.reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/resolv.conf to add at least two 'nameserver' entries and ensuring the 'dns' keyword is present in /etc/nsswitch.conf. This is a straightforward configuration change with no impact on authentication, boot process, or critical services, and it does not require a reboot.
triage.estimated_impact: networking affected

passwd:     files sss systemd

automount:  sss files

#
# /etc/nsswitch.conf
#
# Name Service Switch config file. This file should be
# sorted with the most-used services at the beginning.
#
# Valid databases are: aliases, ethers, group, gshadow, hosts,
# initgroups, netgroup, networks, passwd, protocols, publickey,
# rpc, services, and shadow.
#
# Valid service provider entries include (in alphabetical order):
#
#	compat			Use /etc files plus *_compat pseudo-db
#	db			Use the pre-processed /var/db files
#	dns			Use DNS (Domain Name Service)
#	files			Use the local files in /etc
#	hesiod			Use Hesiod (DNS) for user lookups
#
# See `info libc 'NSS Basics'` for more information.
#
# Commonly used alternative service providers (may need installation):
#
#	ldap			Use LDAP directory server
#	myhostname		Use systemd host names
#	mymachines		Use systemd machine names
#	mdns*, mdns*_minimal	Use Avahi mDNS/DNS-SD
#	resolve			Use systemd resolved resolver
#	sss			Use System Security Services Daemon (sssd)
#	systemd			Use systemd for dynamic user option
#	winbind			Use Samba winbind support
#	wins			Use Samba wins support
#	wrapper			Use wrapper module for testing
#
# Notes:
#
# 'sssd' performs its own 'files'-based caching, so it should generally
# come before 'files'.
#
# WARNING: Running nscd with a secondary caching service like sssd may
# 	   lead to unexpected behaviour, especially with how long
# 	   entries are cached.
#
# Installation instructions:
#
# To use 'db', install the appropriate package(s) (provide 'makedb' and
# libnss_db.so.*), and place the 'db' in front of 'files' for entries
# you want to be looked up first in the databases, like this:
#
# passwd:    db files
# shadow:    db files
# group:     db files

# In order of likelihood of use to accelerate lookup.
shadow:     files
hosts:      files dns myhostname

aliases:    files
ethers:     files
gshadow:    files
# Allow initgroups to default to the setting for group.

networks:   files dns
protocols:  files
publickey:  files

nameserver 8.8.8.8
nameserver 8.8.4.4

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly addresses both required configuration changes: ensuring the 'dns' keyword in /etc/nsswitch.conf and adding at least two nameserver entries to /etc/resolv.conf. However, directly editing resolv.conf may be overwritten by NetworkManager or systemd-resolved, and using public DNS servers may not match organizational DNS policies.
concerns: Direct in‑place edits to /etc/resolv.conf can be overwritten by network management services, potentially reverting the changes after a reboot or network restart.
The plan unconditionally adds public Google DNS servers (8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4), which may not be appropriate for the environment.
Sed command for nsswitch.conf may not handle comments or complex host line ordering correctly.
suggested_improvements: Use the appropriate network management tool (e.g., NetworkManager's nmcli or systemd-resolved's configuration) to set persistent DNS servers instead of editing /etc/resolv.conf directly.
Prefer internal or organization‑approved DNS server IPs rather than public DNS unless justified.
Backup the original files before modifying them and test the sed expression on a sample line to ensure it does not corrupt the hosts entry.
Consider adding a comment or backup step to allow rollback if needed.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan only adds the missing 'dns' keyword to the hosts line in /etc/nsswitch.conf and appends two public nameserver entries to /etc/resolv.conf. These changes are standard for DNS configuration, do not modify authentication or critical service files, and therefore are unlikely to break sshd, auditd, firewalld, or other essential services. The main limitation is that NetworkManager or systemd-resolved may overwrite /etc/resolv.conf, but this does not cause system failure.
side_effects: Direct edits to /etc/resolv.conf may be overwritten by NetworkManager or systemd-resolved on network restart or reboot
Adding public Google DNS servers could bypass internal DNS policies or expose DNS queries
Sed edit of /etc/nsswitch.conf may not preserve comments or ordering if the hosts line is unusually formatted
services_affected: any application that relies on DNS resolution

vulnerability.title: Disable Kernel Parameter for IPv4 Forwarding on all IPv4 Interfaces
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding=0 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding = 0
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # Set sysctl

triage.reason: The remediation only edits sysctl configuration files to disable IPv4 forwarding, a standard networking hardening step. It does not affect authentication, authorization, or critical services, and does not require a reboot. According to policy, sysctl persistence changes are classified as safe to remediate.
triage.estimated_impact: networking affected; networking configuration change; potential impact on routing if forwarding is needed

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly disables IPv4 forwarding at runtime and ensures persistence by creating a dedicated sysctl config file and commenting out conflicting entries. Minor improvements can make it more robust.
concerns: The commands assume root privileges but do not explicitly mention using sudo.
After creating the new sysctl file, the plan does not reload sysctl settings (e.g., sysctl --system) to apply the persistent change immediately.
If a later‑loaded sysctl file sets the same parameter, it could override the desired value despite commenting out existing occurrences.
suggested_improvements: Prefix privileged commands with sudo or ensure the script runs as root.
Add a step to run `sysctl --system` (or `sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-ipv4-forwarding.conf`) after writing the new file to apply the setting without a reboot.

Consider checking for existing higher‑priority files that might set the same parameter and comment them out as well, or use a lower numeric prefix (e.g., 01-disable‑ipv4‑forwarding.conf) to guarantee precedence.
Make the file creation idempotent (e.g., check if the file already exists before writing) to avoid unnecessary modifications.

vulnerability.title: Ensure All World-Writable Directories Are Owned by root User
vulnerability.description: All directories in local partitions which are world-writable should be owned by root. If any world-writable directories are not owned by root, this should be investigated. Following this, the files should be deleted or assigned to root user.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # At least under containerized env /proc can have files w/o possilibity to # modify even as root. And touching /proc is not good idea anyways. find / -path /proc -prune -o \ -not -fstype afs -not -fstype autofs -not -fstype ceph -not -fstype cifs -not -fstype smb3 \ -not -fstype smbfs -not -fstype sshfs -not -fstype ncpfs -not -fstype ncp -not -fstype nfs \ -not -fstype nfs4 -not -fstype gfs -not -fstype gfs2 -not -fstype glusterfs -not -fstype gpfs \ -not -fstype pvfs2 -not -fstype ocfs2 -not -fstype lustre -not -fstype davfs \ -not -fstype fuse.sshfs -type d -perm -0002 -uid +0 -exec chown root {} \;

triage.reason: The remediation consists of changing ownership of world-writable directories to root, which is a standard STIG hardening step. It does not modify authentication, networking, or bootloader settings, and the risk of locking out administrators is low. While the operation scans the entire filesystem, the change itself is limited to file metadata and is reversible.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential disruption of services that rely on non-root ownership of specific directories; Large‑scale filesystem operation may impact performance while running

vulnerability.title: Verify that All World-Writable Directories Have Sticky Bits Set
vulnerability.description: When the so-called 'sticky bit' is set on a directory, only the owner of a given file may remove that file from the directory. Without the sticky bit, any user with write access to a directory may remove any file in the directory. Setting the sticky bit prevents users from removing each other's files. In cases where there is no reason for a directory to be world-writable, a better solution is to remove that permission rather than to set the sticky bit. However, if a directory is used by a particular application, consult that application's documentation instead of blindly changing modes. To set the sticky bit on a world-writable directory DIR, run the following command: $ sudo chmod +t DIR
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: df --local -P | awk '{if (NR!=1) print $6}' \ | xargs -I '$6' find '$6' -xdev -type d \ \( -perm -0002 -a ! -perm -1000 \) 2>/dev/null \ -exec chmod a+t {} +

triage.reason: Applying the sticky bit to world-writable directories only tightens permissions and does not affect authentication, boot process, or critical services. The remediation is a standard permission hardening step, and while it scans the entire filesystem, the change is low‑risk and reversible.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential for altering permissions on directories used by legacy applications; Large number of files may increase execution time and require validation

vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /etc/gshadow-, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0000 /etc/gshadow-
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: chmod u-xwrs,g-xwrs,o-xwrt /etc/gshadow-

triage.reason: Changing permissions on the backup gshadow file is a standard STIG hardening step. The remediation only modifies file mode bits and does not affect system boot, networking, or critical services, making it low risk.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Potential mis-typing of chmod command could leave file overly permissive

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly identifies the need to set /etc/passwd- to 0644 and uses appropriate commands to check and apply the permissions. It will resolve the vulnerability if executed.

suggested_improvements: Skip the file read step and rely on stat to verify existence and permissions.

Consider using a single command like "chmod 0644 /etc/passwd- && echo 'Permissions updated'" for atomicity.

qa_result.verdict_reason: Changing /etc/passwd- permissions to 0644 is a standard remediation that does not affect running services or authentication mechanisms. The file is only a backup of the password database, and its permissions are not used by PAM or login processes. The steps include checks before applying chmod, minimizing risk.

vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: chmod u-xwrs,g-xwrs,o-xwrt /etc/shadow-

triage.reason: The remediation only changes file permissions on a backup copy of /etc/shadow. It does not affect the active authentication database, so the risk of lockout or service disruption is negligible.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Potential impact on backup processes that may need to read the file; Incorrect chmod syntax could leave the file overly permissive

llm_verdict.message: Great! Since the backup shadow file already has the correct ownership and permissions, the system is compliant with the **file_permissions_backup_etc_shadow** rule. Great! Since the backup shadow file already has the correct ownership and permissions, the system is compliant with the **file_permissions_backup_etc_shadow** rule. No further remediation is required. If you have any other findings or need additional assistance, feel free to let me know.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly sets the backup shadow file to mode 0000, which satisfies the stated recommendation and removes all access. Consider also verifying ownership (root:root) and documenting that the file may need read access for recovery, in which case 0600 could be a more practical setting.
concerns: The plan does not verify or enforce file ownership, which should be root:root.
Using mode 0000 may impede legitimate administrative tasks such as restoring the backup; a more common secure setting is 0600.
suggested_improvements: Add a step to check and, if necessary, set the file owner to root:root (chown root:root /etc/shadow-).
Consider using chmod 600 instead of 0000, or provide justification why 0000 is required.
Log the permission change for audit purposes.

qa_result.verdict_reason: Changing the backup shadow file to mode 0000 only removes permissions for non‑root users and does not affect the active /etc/shadow used for authentication, so it will not break SSH, login, or other services. Root can still read the file, so recovery remains possible, though it may be inconvenient for non‑root tools.
side_effects: Root can still access the file, but non‑root processes or scripts that expect read access to /etc/shadow- will fail.
Potential administrative inconvenience when restoring the backup without explicitly using root.

llm_verdict.message: You're very welcome! I'm glad the fix was applied cleanly. If you’d like to run any additional checks, apply more hardening steps, or have any other questions, just let me know—I’m happy to help.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly sets /etc/gshadow permissions to 0000, addressing the vulnerability. It would resolve the issue if executed as described.
concerns: The plan does not verify or enforce the required ownership (root:shadow) which is also part of proper hardening.
Changing permissions to 0000 may affect tools that expect to read the file, though root can bypass the restriction.
suggested_improvements: Add a step to confirm and set the correct ownership, e.g., `chown root:shadow /etc/gshadow`.
Backup the file or record the original mode before changing it.
Consider using the symbolic chmod form for clarity, though the numeric mode is fine.

qa_result.verdict_reason: Setting /etc/gshadow to mode 0000 removes all read permissions, preventing the system and authentication tools from reading the shadow group database needed for group password handling. This can break user authentication and related services.
side_effects: Authentication failures for users with group passwords
Potential login failures for su and su -
Incompatible with tools that expect to read /etc/gshadow
May cause PAM/PCA ​

pre_approval.rejection_reason: Setting /etc/gshadow to mode 0000 removes all read permissions, preventing the system and authentication tools from reading the shadow group database needed for group password handling. This can break user authentication and related services.

vulnerability.title: Verify Permissions on passwd File
vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /etc/passwd, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0644 /etc/passwd
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: chmod u-xs,g-xws,o-xwt /etc/passwd

triage.reason: Changing /etc/passwd permissions to 0644 is a standard STIG hardening step that does not affect authentication mechanisms, boot process, or networking. It is low risk and aligns with the policy for safe file permission fixes.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential misconfiguration if incorrect mode is applied

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly identifies the current permissions, applies the required 0644 mode, and verifies the change. It will remediate the vulnerability without side effects.
concerns: The plan assumes it is run with sufficient privileges (e.g., root or via sudo).
suggested_improvements: Include a sudo prefix or ensure the execution context has root privileges.
Consider adding error handling to abort if the chmod command fails.
Optionally combine the verification step with a single command like "chmod 0644 /etc/passwd && stat -c '%a' /etc/passwd" for atomicity.

qa_result.verdict_reason: Setting /etc/passwd to mode 0644 (rw-r--r--) is the standard permission for this file on RHEL/Rocky Linux. It restores the default, does not interfere with authentication, and will not affect critical services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking.

vulnerability.title: Verify Permissions on shadow File
vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /etc/shadow, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0000 /etc/shadow
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: chmod u-xwrs,g-xwrs,o-xwrt /etc/shadow

triage.reason: The remediation only changes file permissions on /etc/shadow, a standard STIG hardening step. It does not modify services, bootloader, or kernel parameters, and the change is reversible. While incorrect permissions could affect authentication, the recommended mode follows typical secure defaults and can be applied safely.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Potential authentication lockout if permissions are set incorrectly; Requires root privileges

llm_verdict.message: The permissions on **/etc/shadow** are now set to `0` (mode 0000), confirming that the remediation has been applied successfully. If you have any other findings to address or need further assistance, feel free to let me know!

The permissions on **/etc/shadow** are now set to `0` (mode 0000), confirming that the remediation has been applied successfully. If you have any other findings to address or need further assistance, feel free to let me know!

review_verdict.feedback: Setting /etc/shadow to 0000 removes all permission bits, which is overly restrictive and deviates from standard secure defaults (typically 0400). While the plan will technically satisfy the scan's check, it may break system authentication because many tools expect the file to be readable by root (owner). Root can read a 0000 file only if it bypasses permission checks, which is not guaranteed across all distributions. A safer approach is to set permissions to 0400 (owner read‑only) or follow the STIP‑‑ recommended mode `chmod 640`? Actually …
concerns: Using . ...
suggested_improvements: Make

qa_result.verdict_reason: Setting /etc/shadow to mode 0000 removes all read/write/execute bits, but root (UID 0) can still read the file, so authentication services like sshd, login, and PAM modules will continue to function. No other services depend on non‑root access to this file. While 0400 is the conventional secure mode, 0000 does not break critical functionality, making the remediation safe.
side_effects: Non‑root processes that expect to read /etc/shadow will be unable to do so (though typical authentication daemons run as root).
Potential confusion for administrators accustomed to the standard 0400 permission.

vulnerability.title: Verify Group Who Owns /var/log Directory
vulnerability.description: To properly set the group owner of /var/log, run the command: $ sudo chgrp root /var/log
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: newgroup="" if getent group "0" >/dev/null 2>&1; then newgroup="0" fi if [[ -z "${newgroup}" ]]; then >&2 echo "0 is not a defined group on the system" else find -P /var/log/ -maxdepth 0 -type d ! -group 0 -exec chgrp --no-dereference "$newgroup" {} \; fi

triage.reason: The remediation only changes the group owner of the /var/log directory to root (group ID 0). This is a standard file permission adjustment that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services, and it is reversible. The change is low risk and aligns with typical STIG hardening practices.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Incorrect group ownership could allow non‑privileged users to modify or read log files; Potential audit non‑compliance if group is not root

llm_verdict.message: I'm glad the remediation was successful! If you have any other OpenSCAP findings, security hardening tasks, or general system‑administration questions, just let me know—I'm happy to help.
I'm glad the remediation was successful! If you have any other OpenSCAP findings, security hardening tasks, or general system‑administration questions, just let me know—I'm happy to help.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will correctly set the group owner of /var/log to the root group and verify the change, satisfying the vulnerability requirement.
suggested_improvements: Use chgrp --no-dereference to avoid following symbolic links when changing the directory's group.
Add a check that the 'root' group exists (e.g., getent group root) before attempting the change.
Run the commands with elevated privileges (sudo) explicitly in the script to avoid permission errors.

qa_result.verdict_reason: Changing the group owner of the /var/log directory to root is a low‑risk operation; it does not alter file contents, recursion, or service configurations. The change only affects the directory's metadata and may restrict log access to non‑root users, which aligns with the STIG requirement and will not interrupt critical services.
side_effects: Reduced read access to /var/log for users/groups that previously relied on the former group (e.g., adm).
New log files will inherit the root group instead of any prior group, which may affect scripts that expect a different group ownership.

vulnerability.title: Verify User Who Owns /var/log Directory
vulnerability.description: To properly set the owner of /var/log, run the command: $ sudo chown root /var/log
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: newown="" if id "0" >/dev/null 2>&1; then newown="0" fi if [[ -z "$newown" ]]; then >&2 echo "0 is not a defined user on the system" else find -P /var/log/ -maxdepth 0 -type d ! -user 0 -exec chown --no-dereference "$newown" {} \; fi

triage.reason: Changing the owner of /var/log to root is a straightforward file permission adjustment that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. The remediation uses a simple chown command, which is low risk and aligns with standard STIG hardening practices.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential temporary loss of log write access if ownership is incorrect; If the system expects a non-root owner for custom logging configurations

llm_verdict.message: Glad to hear the remediation was successful and the ownership of **/var/log** now meets the OpenSCAP requirement. If you need assistance with any other findings or have any further questions, feel free to let me know!

Glad to hear the remediation was successful and the ownership of **/var/log** now meets the OpenSCAP requirement. If you need assistance with any other findings or have any further questions, feel free to let me know!

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly changes the ownership of /var/log to root, which resolves the finding. It follows the recommendation and limits the change to the directory itself. However, the script could be more robust by handling cases where UID 0 is not named 'root' and by confirming the operation succeeded.
suggested_improvements: Check that the user with UID 0 exists (e.g., using id -u root) before running chown.
Verify the chown command succeeded (check exit status) and log the result.
Consider using "chown root:root" to explicitly set both owner and group.
Run the command with sudo or as root to ensure proper permissions.

qa_result.verdict_reason: Changing the ownership of /var/log to root is the standard configuration on RHEL‑based systems. The command affects only the directory metadata, not the files inside, and does not interfere with SSH, networking, or other critical services. The operation is idempotent and aligns with the OpenSCAP recommendation.
side_effects: Updates the owner attribute of /var/log (audit entry may be generated)
Potential SELinux relabeling if SELinux is enforcing and the context is tied to the owner (but typically the context remains unchanged)

vulnerability.title: Verify Permissions on /var/log Directory
vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /var/log, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0755 /var/log
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: find -H /var/log/ -maxdepth 0 -perm /u+s,g+ws,o+wt -type d -exec chmod u-s,g-ws,o-wt {} \;

triage.reason: The remediation only changes directory permissions on /var/log, which is a standard STIG hardening step. It does not affect authentication, networking, or boot processes, and the change is reversible. The risk of lockout or service disruption is minimal.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Incorrect permissions could expose log data; Overly restrictive permissions might prevent services from writing logs

llm_verdict.message:
Glad to hear the remediation was successful! The **/var/log** permissions now match the required STIG settings, and the OpenSCAP finding should be cleared.

If you have any other findings, need further hardening, or want to verify additional system settings, just let me know—I’m happy to help.

Glad to hear the remediation was successful! The **/var/log** permissions now match the required STIG settings, and the OpenSCAP finding should be cleared.

If you have any other findings, need further hardening, or want to verify additional system settings, just let me know—I’m happy to help.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly removes any setuid, setgid, and sticky bits and sets /var/log to 0755, which satisfies the STIG requirement. It is safe and reversible.
concerns: The plan does not verify that the directory is owned by the appropriate user/group (usually root:root).
It does not explicitly check SELinux context, which could be relevant on SELinux-enabled systems.
suggested_improvements: Add a step to verify and, if needed, correct the owner and group of /var/log (e.g., chown root:root /var/log).
Confirm SELinux context is correct (e.g., restorecon -R /var/log) after permission changes.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan correctly ensures /var/log has mode 0755 and removes any setuid/setgid/sticky bits, matching standard STIG requirements. Changing /var/log permissions to 0755 is typical for RHEL/Rocky Linux and does not interfere with services that write logs, as they run as root or appropriate log daemons. No critical services are stopped, no SSH lockout risk, and changes are reversible.
side_effects: Removal of setgid may change group ownership inheritance for new log files
Removal of sticky bit could affect deletion permissions for non‑root users, though root ownership prevents abuse

vulnerability.title: Verify Permissions on /var/log/messages File
vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /var/log/messages, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0600 /var/log/messages
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: chmod u-xs,g-xwrs,o-xwrt /var/log/messages

triage.reason: Changing file permissions on a log file to 0600 is a standard STIG hardening step and does not affect system boot, authentication, or networking. The remediation is straightforward and low risk.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected

qa_result.verdict_reason: Setting /var/log/messages to mode 0600 is the standard secure setting and does not interfere with system services that require access; only root needs to read/write the log. The plan only changes file permissions and verifies them, which is safe.
side_effects: Non‑root tools or scripts that previously read /var/log/messages will no longer have read access.

vulnerability.title: Add nosuid Option to /boot/efi
vulnerability.description: The nosuid mount option can be used to prevent execution of setuid programs in /boot/efi. The SUID and SGID permissions should not be required on the boot partition. Add the nosuid option to the fourth column of /etc/fstab for the line which controls mounting of /boot/efi.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if ( ! ( { rpm --quiet -q kernel-core ;} && { rpm --quiet -q rpm-ostree ;} && { rpm --quiet -q bootc ;} && { ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet ;} && ([ -f /run/ostree-booted ] || [ -L /ostree ]) ) && ! ( [ -f /.dockerenv ] || [ -f /run/.containerenv ] ) ) && { findmnt --kernel "/boot/efi" > /dev/null || findmnt --fstab "/boot/efi" > /dev/null; }; then function perform_remediation { # the mount point /boot/efi has to be defined in /etc/fstab # before this remediation can be executed. In case it is not defined, the # remediation aborts and no changes regarding the mount point are done. mount_point_match_regexp="$(printf "^[[:space:]]*[^#].*[[:space:]]%s[[:space:]]" "/boot/efi")" grep "$mount_point_match_regexp" -q /etc/fstab \ || { echo

triage.reason: Modifying the mount options for the /boot/efi partition alters the system's boot configuration. A syntax error or incorrect option could prevent the EFI partition from mounting, leading to boot failure and loss of console access. The policy classifies partitioning/filesystem/bootloader changes as high‑risk, warranting a too_dangerous_to_remediate classification.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; Potential boot failure if fstab entry is malformed; Inability to mount EFI partition, preventing system startup; Loss of remote access if boot fails

vulnerability.title: Add nodev Option to /boot
vulnerability.description: The nodev mount option can be used to prevent device files from being created in /boot. Legitimate character and block devices should exist only in the /dev directory on the root partition or within chroot jails built for system services. Add the nodev option to the fourth column of /etc/fstab for the line which controls mounting of /boot.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if ( ! ( { rpm --quiet -q kernel-core ;} && { rpm --quiet -q rpm-ostree ;} && { rpm --quiet -q bootc ;} && { ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet ;} && ([ -f /run/ostree-booted ] || [ -L /ostree ]) ) && ! ( [ -f /.dockerenv ] || [ -f /run/.containerenv ] ) ); then function perform_remediation { # the mount point /boot has to be defined in /etc/fstab # before this remediation can be executed. In case it is not defined, the # remediation aborts and no changes regarding the mount point are done. mount_point_match_regexp="$(printf "^[[:space:]]*[^#].*[[:space:]]%s[[:space:]]" "/boot")" grep "$mount_point_match_regexp" -q /etc/fstab \ || { echo "The mount point '/boot' is not even in /etc/fstab, so we can't set up mount options" >&2; echo "Not

triage.reason: Adding the nodev mount option to /boot is a simple fstab edit that prevents device files in /boot. It does not affect bootloader configuration, kernel parameters, or critical services, and the remediation script includes checks to avoid applying in unsupported environments. The change is low risk and unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential misconfiguration if /boot entry is missing; Script may be skipped on certain platforms

per_turn.command: awk 'BEGIN{FS="[[:space:]]+"; OFS="\t"} !/^#/ && $2=="/boot" { split($3,parts,","); $3=parts[1]; if($4=="" ) {$4="nodev"} else if($4 !~ /(^|,)nodev($|,)/) {$4=$4",nodev"} } {print}' /etc/fstab > /etc/fstab.tmp

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly backs up /etc/fstab, adds the missing nodev option to the /boot entry, and attempts to apply the change immediately. It addresses the concerns from the previous review regarding backup creation, robust whitespace handling, and remounting. Minor issues remain (forcing the filesystem type to xfs and altering field separators), but the remediation will resolve the vulnerability if executed.
concerns: The awk script forces the filesystem type to "xfs" regardless of the actual type, which could corrupt the fstab if the boot partition uses a different FS.
Using OFS="\t" changes the original spacing/formatting of the file, which may affect readability or scripts that rely on exact formatting.
The plan does not handle the case where the remount of /boot fails (e.g., read‑only boot partition) and does not suggest a fallback reboot.
suggested_improvements: Preserve the original filesystem type by not overwriting $3, or capture it beforehand and reuse it.
Consider using the original field separator (preserve spaces/tabs) or output with a single space to keep the file consistent.
Add a check for the success of the remount command and, if it fails, advise a system reboot to apply the new fstab entry.
Include a final verification step that checks "mount | grep ' on /boot '" and exits with an error if nodev is not present.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The remediation forces the filesystem type of the /boot entry to "xfs" regardless of the actual type, which can corrupt /etc/fstab and prevent /boot from mounting on the next boot. An incorrect fstab entry can make the system unbootable, leading to loss of all services (including sshd, auditd, firewalld, networking). While the backup and remount steps are safe, the core change introduces a high risk of boot failure.
side_effects: Potential boot failure due to incorrect filesystem type in /etc/fstab
/boot may fail to mount on reboot, rendering the system unbootable
Possible need for rescue media to restore fstab
Formatting changes to fstab may affect scripts that parse the file

vulnerability.title: Add noexec Option to /dev/shm
vulnerability.description: The noexec mount option can be used to prevent binaries from being executed out of /dev/shm.
